name: Tool
summary: This class is abstract. Implement the methods described in this class to create a tool. You can not sub-class this class because it is not defined by the API. Tool is the interface that you implement to create a SketchUp tool. See our code example for how to create a custom tool in Ruby. To create a new tool in Ruby, you must define a new class that implements the methods for the events that you want to respond to. You do not have to implement methods for every possible event that a Tool can respond to. Once you have defined a tool class, you select that tool by creating an instance of it and passing it to Model#select_tool. For example: The following table contains several constants you can use when check for certain key presses inside the keyboard handling callbacks: CONSTRAIN_MODIFIER_KEY = Shift Key CONSTRAIN_MODIFIER_MASK = Shift Key COPY_MODIFIER_KEY = Alt/Option on Mac, Ctrl on PC COPY_MODIFIER_MASK = Alt/Option on Mac, Ctrl on PC ALT_MODIFIER_KEY = Command on Mac, Alt on PC ALT_MODIFIER_MASK = Command on Mac, Alt on PC

methods.name: activate
methods.summary: The #activate method is called by SketchUp when the tool is selected.
methods.remarks: The #activate method is called by SketchUp when the tool is selected. It is a good place to put most of your initialization, such as instance variables to track the state of the tool.

methods.name: deactivate
methods.summary: The #deactivate method is called when the tool is deactivated because a different tool was selected.
methods.remarks: The #deactivate method is called when the tool is deactivated because a different tool was selected.

methods.name: draw
methods.summary: Note: If you draw outside the model bounds you need to implement #getExtents which return a bounding box large enough to include the points you draw.
methods.remarks: Note: If you draw outside the model bounds you need to implement #getExtents which return a bounding box large enough to include the points you draw. Otherwise your drawing will be clipped. The #draw method is called by SketchUp whenever the view is refreshed to allow the tool to do its own drawing. If the tool has some temporary graphics that it wants displayed while it is active, it should implement this method and draw to the View.

params.name: view
params.doc: A View object where the method was invoked.

methods.name: enableVCB?
methods.summary: The #enableVCB? method is used to tell SketchUp whether to allow the user to enter text into the VCB (value control box, aka the “measurements” panel).
methods.remarks: The #enableVCB? method is used to tell SketchUp whether to allow the user to enter text into the VCB (value control box, aka the “measurements” panel). If you do not implement this method, then the vcb is disabled by default.

returns.doc: Return true if you want the VCB enabled

methods.name: getExtents
methods.summary: In order to accurately draw things, SketchUp needs to know the extents of what it is drawing.
methods.remarks: In order to accurately draw things, SketchUp needs to know the extents of what it is drawing. If the tool is doing its own drawing, it may need to implement this method to tell SketchUp the extents of what it will be drawing. If you don't implement this method, you may find that part of what the tool is drawing gets clipped to the extents of the rest of the model.

methods.name: getInstructorContentDirectory
methods.summary: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2014 this method would assume the path was relative to the SketchUp resource folder.
methods.remarks: Note: Prior to SketchUp 2014 this method would assume the path was relative to the SketchUp resource folder. From 2014 and onwards you can specify the absolute path to an HTML file or the absolute path to a directory containing an index.html file. The #getInstructorContentDirectory method is used to tell SketchUp the directory containing your Tool's instructor content. To use this, create a custom instructor directory, put an index.html file inside of it, and then return that path via this method. If the SketchUp user has the Instructor window open when they activate your tool, they will see your html file.

returns.doc: the directory path where the Instructor content exists.

methods.name: getMenu
methods.signature: getMenu(menu) => nil | getMenu(menu, flags, x, y, view) => nil | getMenu(menu, flags, x, y, view) => nil
methods.summary: Note: In SketchUp 2015 the flags, x, y and view parameters were added.
methods.remarks: Note: In SketchUp 2015 the flags, x, y and view parameters were added. They are needed if you need to pick the entities under the mouse position. The new parameters are optional, but if you need to use one you must include them all. The #getMenu method is called by SketchUp to let the tool provide its own context menu. Most tools will not want to implement this method and, instead, use the normal context menu found on all entities. If you do implement this method, the argument is a Menu. You should use the Menu#add_item method to build the context menu. Your tool will use a standard context menu by default if you do not implement this method. Implement this method if you want a context-click to display something other than this default context menu.

methods.name: onCancel
methods.summary: Note: When something is undone #onCancel is called before the undo is actually executed.
methods.remarks: Note: When something is undone #onCancel is called before the undo is actually executed. If you need to do something with the model after an undo use ModelObserver#onTransactionUndo. Note: When #onKeyDown is implemented and returns true, pressing Esc doesn't trigger #onCancel. The #onCancel method is called by SketchUp to cancel the current operation of the tool. The typical response will be to reset the tool to its initial state. The reason identifies the action that triggered the call. The reason can be one of the following values: 0: the user canceled the current operation by hitting the escape key. 1: the user re-selected the same tool from the toolbar or menu. 2: the user did an undo while the tool was active.

methods.name: onKeyDown
methods.summary: The #onKeyDown method is called by SketchUp when the user presses a key on the keyboard.
methods.remarks: The #onKeyDown method is called by SketchUp when the user presses a key on the keyboard. If you want to get input from the VCB, you should implement onUserText rather than this method. This method is can be used for special keys such as the Shift key, Ctrl key, and so on, or for just determining which key a user pressed. This method is actually called for all keys that are pressed. There are several “virtual keys” defined as constants you can use. Their use is cross platform. They are: VK_ALT VK_COMMAND VK_CONTROL VK_DELETE VK_DOWN VK_END VK_HOME VK_INSERT VK_LEFT VK_MENU VK_NEXT VK_PRIOR VK_RIGHT VK_SHIFT VK_SPACE VK_UP V6: There is a bug on Windows where the typematic effect does not work. Typematic effects work fine on a Mac.

params.name: repeat
params.doc: A value of 1 for a single press of a key. A value of 2 if the user has pressed a key and is holding it down.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys at the time of the event.

returns.doc: Return true to prevent SketchUp from processing the event.

methods.name: onKeyUp
methods.summary: The #onKeyUp method is called by SketchUp when the user releases a key on the keyboard.
methods.remarks: The #onKeyUp method is called by SketchUp when the user releases a key on the keyboard.

params.name: repeat
params.doc: A value of 1 for a single press of a key. A value of 2 if the user has pressed a key and is holding it down.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys at the time of the event.

returns.doc: Return true to prevent SketchUp from processing the event.

methods.name: onLButtonDoubleClick
methods.signature: onLButtonDoubleClick(flags, x, y, view) => Object | onLButtonDoubleClick(flags, x, y, view) => Object
methods.summary: The #onLButtonDoubleClick is called by SketchUp when the user double clicks with the left mouse button.
methods.remarks: The #onLButtonDoubleClick is called by SketchUp when the user double clicks with the left mouse button.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

methods.name: onLButtonDown
methods.signature: onLButtonDown(flags, x, y, view) => Object | onLButtonDown(flags, x, y, view) => Object
methods.summary: The #onLButtonDown method is called by SketchUp when the left mouse button is pressed.
methods.remarks: The #onLButtonDown method is called by SketchUp when the left mouse button is pressed. Most tools will implement this method.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

methods.name: onLButtonUp
methods.signature: onLButtonUp(flags, x, y, view) => Object | onLButtonUp(flags, x, y, view) => Object
methods.summary: The #onLButtonUp method is called by SketchUp when the left mouse button is released.
methods.remarks: The #onLButtonUp method is called by SketchUp when the left mouse button is released.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

methods.name: onMButtonDoubleClick
methods.signature: onMButtonDoubleClick(flags, x, y, view) => Object | onMButtonDoubleClick(flags, x, y, view) => Object
methods.summary: Note: Though this method has been documented in the Ruby API for many years, it has never worked properly.
methods.remarks: Note: Though this method has been documented in the Ruby API for many years, it has never worked properly. We are leaving this documentation in place for now in the hopes of fixing the implementation, but you won't have any luck trying to use it in SU7 and earlier. The #onMButtonDoubleClick method is called by SketchUp when the middle mouse button (on a three button mouse) is double-clicked. Only implement this method if you want SketchUp to react to a middle mouse button being double-clicked.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

methods.name: onMButtonDown
methods.signature: onMButtonDown(flags, x, y, view) => Object | onMButtonDown(flags, x, y, view) => Object
methods.summary: The #onMButtonDown method is called by SketchUp when the middle mouse button (on a three button mouse) is down.
methods.remarks: The #onMButtonDown method is called by SketchUp when the middle mouse button (on a three button mouse) is down. The Orbit tool is activated by default when the middle mouse button is down. Implement this method if you want a middle mouse button to do something other than invoke the Orbit tool.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

methods.name: onMButtonUp
methods.signature: onMButtonUp(flags, x, y, view) => Object | onMButtonUp(flags, x, y, view) => Object
methods.summary: The #onMButtonUp method is called by SketchUp when the middle mouse button (on a three button mouse) is released.
methods.remarks: The #onMButtonUp method is called by SketchUp when the middle mouse button (on a three button mouse) is released. SketchUp returns to the previous tool from the Orbit tool when the middle mouse button is released. Implement this method if you want a middle mouse button to do something other than return to the previous tool when in the Orbit tool.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

methods.name: onMouseEnter
methods.summary: The #onMouseEnter method is called by SketchUp when the mouse enters the viewport.
methods.remarks: The #onMouseEnter method is called by SketchUp when the mouse enters the viewport.

methods.name: onMouseLeave
methods.summary: The #onMouseLeave method is called by SketchUp when the mouse leaves the viewport.
methods.remarks: The #onMouseLeave method is called by SketchUp when the mouse leaves the viewport.

methods.name: onMouseMove
methods.signature: onMouseMove(flags, x, y, view) => Object | onMouseMove(flags, x, y, view) => Object
methods.summary: The #onMouseMove method is called by SketchUp whenever the mouse is moved.
methods.remarks: The #onMouseMove method is called by SketchUp whenever the mouse is moved. You will often want to implement this method. Try to make this method as efficient as possible because this method is called often.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

methods.name: onMouseWheel
methods.signature: onMouseWheel(flags, delta, x, y, view) => Boolean | onMouseWheel(flags, delta, x, y, view) => Boolean
methods.summary: The #onMouseWheel method is called by SketchUp when the mouse scroll wheel is used.
methods.remarks: The #onMouseWheel method is called by SketchUp when the mouse scroll wheel is used.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

params.name: delta
params.doc: Either 1 or -1 depending on which direction the mouse wheel scrolled.

returns.doc: Return true to prevent SketchUp from performing default zoom action.

methods.name: onRButtonDoubleClick
methods.signature: onRButtonDoubleClick(flags, x, y, view) => Object | onRButtonDoubleClick(flags, x, y, view) => Object
methods.summary: The #onRButtonDoubleClick is called by SketchUp when the user double clicks with the right mouse button.
methods.remarks: The #onRButtonDoubleClick is called by SketchUp when the user double clicks with the right mouse button.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

methods.name: onRButtonDown
methods.signature: onRButtonDown(flags, x, y, view) => Object | onRButtonDown(flags, x, y, view) => Object
methods.summary: The #onRButtonDown method is called by SketchUp when the user presses the right mouse button.
methods.remarks: The #onRButtonDown method is called by SketchUp when the user presses the right mouse button. Implement this method, along with the tool.getMenu method, when you want your tool to do something other than display the default context menu when the right mouse button is clicked.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

methods.name: onRButtonUp
methods.signature: onRButtonUp(flags, x, y, view) => Object | onRButtonUp(flags, x, y, view) => Object
methods.summary: The #onRButtonUp method is called by SketchUp when the user releases the right mouse button.
methods.remarks: The #onRButtonUp method is called by SketchUp when the user releases the right mouse button.

params.name: flags
params.doc: A bit mask that tells the state of the modifier keys and other mouse buttons at the time.

methods.name: onReturn
methods.summary: The #onReturn method is called by SketchUp when the user hit the Return key to complete an operation in the tool.
methods.remarks: The #onReturn method is called by SketchUp when the user hit the Return key to complete an operation in the tool. This method will rarely need to be implemented.

methods.name: onSetCursor
methods.summary: The #onSetCursor method is called by SketchUp when the tool wants to set the cursor.
methods.remarks: The #onSetCursor method is called by SketchUp when the tool wants to set the cursor.

returns.doc: Return true to prevent SketchUp using the default cursor.

methods.name: onUserText
methods.summary: The #onUserText method is called by SketchUp when the user has typed text into the VCB and hit return.
methods.remarks: The #onUserText method is called by SketchUp when the user has typed text into the VCB and hit return.

params.name: text
params.doc: The text string that was typed into the VCB.

params.name: view
params.doc: A view object where the method was invoked.

methods.name: resume
methods.summary: The #resume method is called by SketchUp when the tool becomes active again after being suspended.
methods.remarks: The #resume method is called by SketchUp when the tool becomes active again after being suspended.

methods.name: suspend
methods.summary: The #suspend method is called by SketchUp when the tool temporarily becomes inactive because another tool has been activated.
methods.remarks: The #suspend method is called by SketchUp when the tool temporarily becomes inactive because another tool has been activated. This typically happens when a viewing tool is activated, such as when orbit is active due to the middle mouse button.
